item.title: Why a $25 federal minimum wage would be a disaster
item.description: Minimum wage, Democratic Socialists' ideas, ‘teen takeovers’ and more, in readers' eyes.

item.title: Why are marriage rates falling?
item.description: Why are young adults increasingly rejecting marriage despite clear data showing it leads to greater happiness and financial success? In this episode of "Make It Make Sense," Kate Andrews and Carine Hajjar sit down with Brad Wilcox, sociology professor at the University of Virginia and a fellow at the American Enterprise Institute and the Institute for Family Studies. They break down the modern "marriage paradox" and debunk the mainstream media's anti-family narrative, explaining why married mothers and fathers report significantly higher levels of meaning, stability and prosperity. The conversation also explores how young men and women can navigate the modern dating scene, the controversial tradwife movement, and the real-world consequences of casual hookup culture.

item.title: Nolan’s gorgeous ‘Odyssey’ makes the controversies evaporate
item.description: Homer asks: How should this story be told? Here’s the version for our times.

item.title: What a death and a withdrawal could mean for the Senate
item.description: Without Lindsey Graham and Graham Platner, both parties face a late-summer scramble.

item.title: Maui went from wildfires to purgatory. Here’s why it struggles to rebuild.
item.description: Rebuilding in the historic town lags because of an unworkable maze of regulations.

item.title: He had an incentive to lie. Then he testified in 37 cases.
item.description: Jailhouse testimony can earn witnesses a lighter sentence. Is that a problem?

item.title: Three-fifths could have a new meaning for Black lawmakers
item.description: The latest political maps will hollow out representation.

item.title: Why Poland could be Trump’s new ally in the G-20
item.description: Trump’s Miami summit is the perfect moment to elevate Warsaw.

item.title: This actor drove 10,000 miles to ask men about loneliness
item.description:
For a lot of men, friendship doesn’t disappear all at once. It fades slowly: a missed call here, a canceled plan there, a few busy years that become a decade. Before you know it, the relationship has moved to the background of life, where it can feel easier to assume the bond will survive on memory than to do the awkward work of reaching back out. Andrew McCarthy wanted to understand why that story is so common.
Host Megan McArdle is joined by the actor, director and author, whose new book, “Who Needs Friends: An Unscientific Examination of Male Friendship Across America,” follows his 10,000-mile journey to reconnect with old friends and ask men what friendship means to them.

item.title: The Potomac River deserves monumental attention
item.description: Domestic migration, IRS penalties, Potomac protections and the Metro, in readers' eyes.

item.title: 175,000 lives should persuade Europe: Hold your nose and install AC
item.description: Air conditioning saves lives when outdoor temperatures are unbearable.

item.title: This bizarre tick-borne illness may be more common than once thought
item.description: Many patients and physicians don't recognize alpha-gal syndrome until years after symptoms begin
